# stlouisbankruptcy.org -- Full Content

This file contains the full text content of all pages on stlouisbankruptcy.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).

## St. Louis Bankruptcy -- Chapter 7 & 13 Filing Guide [2026]
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

St. Louis Bankruptcy -- Your Complete Filing Guide for 2026

You are not alone. Over 58,254 St. Louis area residents have filed for bankruptcy since 2008. This free guide will help you understand your options, know what to expect, and make informed decisions.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

58,254

E.D. Mo. Cases (2008-2024)

61.9%

Dismissal Rate

38.1%

Discharge Rate

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 -- Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 -- Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Eastern District of Missouri at a Glance

Understanding local court data can help you make more informed decisions about filing bankruptcy in St. Louis.

E.D. Mo. Dismissal Rate

61.9% dismissed

Out of 58,254 Chapter 13 cases filed between 2008 and 2024, 61.9% were dismissed before plan completion. Only 38.1% achieved a successful discharge.

Courthouse: 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

Key Metrics

41.3%

Prior filer rate -- 41.3% of filers had a previous bankruptcy case. Pro se rate: 2.8%. Estimated 1328(f) violations: 2,393.

View full statistics →

What to Expect When Filing

The Process at a Glance

Credit counseling -- You must complete a credit counseling course within 180 days before filing. Cost: about $15-25.

File your petition -- Your attorney files the petition, schedules, and statements with the court. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, you attend a brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education course -- You must complete a second financial education course before discharge. Cost: about $10-25.

Discharge -- Chapter 7: about 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Detailed timeline and what to expect →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost?

The total cost depends on the chapter you file and your specific situation:

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000 - $2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500 - $4,000 for Chapter 13 in St. Louis. Be cautious of firms charging significantly less -- learn how to spot a bankruptcy mill in St. Louis

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in St. Louis?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in St. Louis typically range from $1,000 - $2,000 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 - $4,000 for Chapter 13. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Missouri?

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your home. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments. See all exemptions.

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy?

Missouri allows a vehicle exemption of $3,000. If your car equity is below that amount, you can keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments. Learn about exemptions.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors usually happens about 30 days after filing. Full timeline.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt quickly but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 allows you to keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Compare chapters.

See all frequently asked questions →

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Need Free Bankruptcy Help?

If you cannot afford a bankruptcy attorney, free help is available. Legal aid organizations, law school clinics, and court self-help programs can assist you at no cost.

Find Free Help in Missouri

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in St. Louis -- Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline | St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in St. Louis

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what St. Louis residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors -- but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

In St. Louis, the vast majority of Chapter 7 filers keep all their property because it falls within Missouri exemption limits.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the state median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility

The median income figures are updated periodically. For a single filer in Missouri, the current median is approximately $55,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

Missouri Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep. Missouri requires you to use state exemptions -- you cannot choose the federal exemption system.

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000 equity

Vehicle$3,000

Household goods$3,000

Tools of trade$3,000

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Public benefitsFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation -- Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing -- Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately -- creditors must stop all collection activity.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting -- Brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education -- Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge -- The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, or debts from fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test for Chapter 7?

The means test compares your household income to the Missouri median. If below, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed calculation determines eligibility.

How long does Chapter 7 take in St. Louis?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7 in Missouri?

Missouri exemptions protect homestead equity up to $15,000, one vehicle up to $3,000, household goods, tools of trade, and retirement accounts. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in St. Louis -- Payment Plans, Dismissal Rates, Timeline | St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/chapter-13.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in St. Louis

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. But in the Eastern District of Missouri, the dismissal rate is significant -- meaning many filers never complete their plan.

61.9% of E.D. Mo. Chapter 13 cases are dismissed

61.9%

E.D. Mo. Dismissal Rate

38.1%

Discharge Rate

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13, sometimes called a "wage earner's plan," allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors according to the plan.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property that would be lost in Chapter 7

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

E.D. Mo. Dismissal Data

A dismissed Chapter 13 means the debtor spent months or years making payments but received no discharge -- no debt relief.

Out of 58,254 Chapter 13 cases filed in the Eastern District of Missouri between 2008 and 2024:

61.9% were dismissed before plan completion

38.1% achieved a successful discharge

41.3% of filers had a prior bankruptcy case

2.8% of filers represented themselves (pro se)

Why it matters: A dismissed Chapter 13 means the automatic stay ends, creditors resume collection, and the debtor receives no discharge. Strong legal representation is critical to completing a Chapter 13 plan. View full statistics.

How a Chapter 13 Plan Works

The Monthly Payment

Your monthly payment is calculated based on your disposable income. The payment must cover:

Priority debts in full: Recent taxes, child support arrears, attorney fees

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage arrears, car loan arrears

Trustee fees: Typically around 10% of plan payments

Unsecured creditors: Whatever remains goes to credit cards, medical bills, etc.

Plan Duration: 3 or 5 Years?

If your income is below the state median, you may be able to complete a 3-year plan. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The court will not approve a plan longer than 5 years.

What Happens at the End?

After completing all plan payments, the court enters a discharge under 11 U.S.C. section 1328. This eliminates remaining eligible unsecured debts.

If you received a prior discharge, time bars under section 1328(f) may prevent you from receiving another discharge.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation, tax returns, and debt records.

Day 1 -- Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments stop immediately.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed (if not filed with the petition).

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing. The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate in St. Louis?

The Eastern District of Missouri has a 61.9% Chapter 13 dismissal rate. These rates matter because a dismissed case means no debt relief despite months or years of payments.

How long is a Chapter 13 repayment plan?

Plans last 3 to 5 years. If your income is below the state median, you may qualify for a 3-year plan. If above, your plan must be 5 years.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. You can keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan. The automatic stay stops foreclosure immediately.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

The automatic stay ends, creditors resume collection, and you receive no discharge. You may be able to file again, but there may be waiting periods.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Missouri Bankruptcy Exemptions -- What You Can Keep | St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/exemptions.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Missouri Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Understanding them is critical -- they can mean the difference between keeping and losing your home, car, and savings.

What Are Exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken to pay your creditors. In Chapter 7, non-exempt property can be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, the value of non-exempt property affects how much you must pay through your plan.

Missouri requires you to use state exemptions -- you cannot choose the federal exemption system.

Missouri Exemptions -- Full List

Homestead -- $15,000

Protects up to $15,000 of equity in your primary residence. Equity is your home's current market value minus what you owe on the mortgage.

RSMo 513.475

Motor Vehicle -- $3,000

Protects up to $3,000 of equity in one motor vehicle. If you owe more than the car is worth (you are "underwater"), there is no equity to protect and the exemption is not needed.

RSMo 513.430(1)

Other Missouri Exemptions

PropertyAmount

Household goods, furnishings, appliances$3,000

Tools of trade, books, equipment$3,000

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)Unlimited

Social Security benefitsUnlimited

Unemployment benefitsUnlimited

Workers' compensationUnlimited

Public assistance benefitsUnlimited

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Missouri homestead exemption?

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your primary residence. Equity is the difference between your home's value and what you owe on the mortgage.

How much vehicle equity can I protect in Missouri?

Missouri's motor vehicle exemption is $3,000. If your car equity is below that amount, you can keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you keep your car regardless. Learn about Chapter 13.

Are retirement accounts protected in Missouri?

Yes. Retirement accounts including IRAs, 401(k)s, and pensions are fully exempt. Social Security benefits are also fully protected under federal law (42 U.S.C. 407).

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in St. Louis? -- Filing Fees, Attorney Fees | St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/cost.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in St. Louis?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

$1,000-$4,000

Typical Attorney Fees

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,000 - $2,000Typical St. Louis range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Total estimate$1,400 - $2,400

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$2,500 - $4,000Typical St. Louis range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Total estimate$2,900 - $4,400Most paid through plan, not upfront

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

A major advantage of Chapter 13 is that attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment, with the rest paid through monthly plan payments over 3-5 years.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri -- provides free bankruptcy representation for qualifying low-income individuals

Fee waiver -- eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments -- pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing -- filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended (only 2.8% of E.D. Mo. filers do this)

Reduced-fee credit counseling -- many approved agencies offer reduced fees for low-income filers

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in St. Louis?

The filing fee is $338. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000 - $2,000. Credit counseling costs about $25-50 total. Total: typically $1,400 - $2,400. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in St. Louis?

The filing fee is $313 and attorney fees typically range from $2,500 - $4,000. Most costs can be paid through your repayment plan.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The filing fee can be waived in Chapter 7 if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Legal Services of Eastern Missouri may provide free representation.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, most attorneys require full payment before filing. For Chapter 13, fees are typically included in your repayment plan.

Automatic Stay

## What to Expect When Filing Bankruptcy -- Timeline, 341 Meeting, Discharge | St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/what-to-expect.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

What to Expect When Filing Bankruptcy

The bankruptcy process can feel overwhelming, but it follows a clear, predictable path. Here is exactly what happens from start to finish.

Chapter 7 Timeline

1-4 weeks before filing

Preparation

Complete a credit counseling course ($15-25). Gather documents: tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements, vehicle titles, mortgage statements, and a list of all debts and assets.

Day 1

Filing Day

Your petition is filed with the Eastern District of Missouri at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. The automatic stay takes effect immediately. All collection calls, lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, and repossessions must stop.

Day 21-40

341 Meeting of Creditors

You attend a brief meeting of creditors. The trustee verifies your identity, asks questions about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Day 21-60

Debtor Education Course

Complete a second financial education course ($10-25). This must be done before the court will grant your discharge.

Day 60-90 (after 341)

Discharge

The court enters your discharge order. Eligible debts are eliminated. You receive a discharge notice in the mail.

Shortly after discharge

Case Closed

The trustee files a final report and the case is closed. Total time from filing: typically 3-4 months.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing

Preparation

Complete credit counseling. Work with your attorney to develop a feasible repayment plan.

Day 1

Filing Day

Petition and proposed plan filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures and repossessions stop immediately.

Day 30

First Plan Payment

Your first monthly payment to the Chapter 13 trustee is due 30 days after filing, even if your plan has not been confirmed yet.

Day 21-40

341 Meeting of Creditors

The trustee reviews your proposed repayment plan and may request modifications.

Day 45-120

Confirmation Hearing

The court decides whether to approve your plan. If approved, you continue monthly payments.

Years 1-5

Plan Payments

Make monthly payments to the trustee for 3-5 years. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

After plan completion

Discharge

Complete debtor education. The court enters a section 1328 discharge.

Important: In the Eastern District of Missouri, 61.9% of Chapter 13 cases are dismissed before plan completion. A dismissed case means no discharge. Strong legal representation is critical.

The Automatic Stay -- Your Immediate Protection

The automatic stay is one of the most powerful protections in bankruptcy law. The moment your petition is filed, creditors must immediately stop:

Phone calls, letters, and emails demanding payment

Lawsuits and court proceedings against you

Wage garnishments

Bank account levies

Foreclosure proceedings

Vehicle repossession

Utility disconnections (for 20 days)

Creditors who violate the automatic stay can be held in contempt and ordered to pay damages.

Exception: If you have had a prior case dismissed within the past year, the automatic stay may be limited to 30 days or may not apply at all. Check your eligibility before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens at the 341 meeting of creditors?

The 341 meeting is a brief hearing where the trustee asks questions under oath. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay takes effect immediately upon filing. It stops collection calls, lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, and repossessions.

How long until I get my discharge?

In Chapter 7, about 3-4 months. In Chapter 13, after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years; Chapter 13 for 7 years. Many filers see scores start recovering within 1-2 years. Learn about rebuilding credit.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## St. Louis Bankruptcy FAQ -- 15 Common Questions Answered | St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/faq.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Frequently Asked Questions

Answers to the 15 most common questions from St. Louis residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in St. Louis?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000 - $2,000 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 - $4,000 for Chapter 13. Full cost breakdown.

What is the means test?

The means test compares your household income to the Missouri median. If below, you qualify for Chapter 7 automatically.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, but there are time limits. Use the 1328(f) screener to check. Learn more.

Property and Assets

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy?

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of home equity. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home and catch up on missed payments. See all exemptions.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy?

Missouri protects $3,000 in vehicle equity. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments. Exemption details.

What happens to my tax refund in bankruptcy?

In Chapter 7, your tax refund may be an asset. In Chapter 13, you may need to turn it over to the trustee. Timing matters -- discuss with your attorney.

The Process

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 takes 3-4 months. Chapter 13 requires 3-5 years. Full timeline.

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing where the trustee asks questions under oath. Most last 5-10 minutes. Full guide.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay takes effect immediately upon filing. It stops lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and collection calls.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay stops garnishments immediately. Learn more.

Types of Debt

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

Will bankruptcy eliminate my student loans?

Generally no, except in cases of undue hardship. Learn more.

Can creditors still call me after I file?

No. The automatic stay prohibits all collection activity.

Life After Bankruptcy

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years; Chapter 13 for 7 years. Many filers see recovery within 1-2 years. Rebuilding credit.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster; Chapter 13 lets you keep property and catch up on debts. Compare them.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## St. Louis Bankruptcy Statistics -- 58,254 Cases Analyzed | St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/statistics.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

St. Louis Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from 58,254 Chapter 13 cases in the Eastern District of Missouri, analyzed from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008-2024).

58,254

Total Ch. 13 Cases (2008-2024)

61.9%

E.D. Mo. Dismissal Rate

38.1%

Discharge Rate

41.3%

Prior Filer Rate

2,393

Est. 1328(f) Violations

2.8%

Pro Se Rate

What These Numbers Mean for You

If you are considering Chapter 13 bankruptcy in St. Louis, these statistics should inform your decision:

61.9% of Chapter 13 cases in E.D. Mo. are dismissed. That means many filers spent months or years making payments and got no debt relief at the end.

41.3% of filers had a previous bankruptcy case. This "prior filer" rate suggests many people are cycling through the system without achieving lasting relief.

An estimated 2,393 cases may involve potential 1328(f) eligibility issues -- filings where the debtor had a prior discharge within the statutory time bar.

Quality of representation matters enormously. The data shows that representation quality is one of the strongest predictors of plan completion.

District Overview

MetricE.D. Mo.

Total Ch. 13 cases (2008-2024)58,254

Dismissal rate61.9%

Discharge rate38.1%

Prior filer rate41.3%

Pro se rate2.8%

Est. 1328(f) violations2,393

Key Takeaways

The Prior Filer Problem

41.3% of E.D. Mo. filers had a prior bankruptcy case. This cycling through the system suggests the first filing failed to provide lasting relief -- and raises questions about whether filers were properly screened under section 1328(f).

2,393 Potential 1328(f) Violations

An estimated 2,393 cases may involve potential eligibility violations under 11 U.S.C. section 1328(f) -- filings where the debtor had a prior discharge within the statutory time bar and could never have received a new discharge. These cases consume court resources and cost consumers time and money for a legally impossible outcome.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many bankruptcy cases have been filed in St. Louis?

Between 2008 and 2024, the Eastern District of Missouri saw 58,254 Chapter 13 filings.

What is the Chapter 13 success rate in St. Louis?

The discharge rate is 38.1% in E.D. Mo. (2008-2024 data). The remaining 61.9% of cases were dismissed.

What does the prior filer rate mean?

41.3% of filers had a previous case. This suggests many are cycling through without lasting relief, and raises questions about 1328(f) eligibility screening.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## St. Louis Bankruptcy Resources -- Courts, Legal Aid, Credit Counseling | St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/resources.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

St. Louis Bankruptcy Resources

Courts, legal aid organizations, credit counseling agencies, and free educational tools to help you navigate the bankruptcy process.

Bankruptcy Court

Eastern District of Missouri -- Bankruptcy Court

Address: 111 S 10th St, Suite 4.625, St. Louis, MO 63102

Phone: (314) 244-4500

Website: www.moeb.uscourts.gov

PACER: ecf.moeb.uscourts.gov

Legal Aid Organizations

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri

Provides free civil legal services to low-income individuals, including bankruptcy representation.

Phone: (314) 534-4200

Website: lsem.org

The Bar Association of Metropolitan St. Louis

Can connect you with attorneys who handle bankruptcy cases, including some who offer reduced fees or payment plans.

Phone: (314) 421-4134

Website: bamsl.org

Credit Counseling and Debtor Education

Two courses are required: a pre-filing credit counseling course and a post-filing debtor education course. You must use agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Approved agencies: justice.gov -- approved agencies

Cost: $15-25 per course (reduced fees available for low-income filers)

Format: Online, phone, or in-person

Time: About 60-90 minutes per course

U.S. Trustee Program

The U.S. Trustee Program oversees the administration of bankruptcy cases.

Region 13: (314) 539-2976

Website: justice.gov/ust

Free Educational Tools

Means Test Explained

National Cost Guide

Dismissal Rate Data

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the St. Louis bankruptcy court?

Eastern District of Missouri: 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. Phone: (314) 244-4500.

Is there free legal help for bankruptcy in St. Louis?

Yes. Legal Services of Eastern Missouri ((314) 534-4200) provides free bankruptcy representation for qualifying low-income individuals.

Where can I take the required credit counseling course?

The U.S. Trustee Program maintains a list at justice.gov/ust. Most offer courses online for $15-25.

## Bankruptcy by County -- St. Louis Metro Area | St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/counties.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Bankruptcy by County -- St. Louis Metro Area

Your county determines which federal court handles your bankruptcy case and which state exemptions protect your property. Find your county below for specific information.

4 Counties in the St. Louis Metro

St. Louis City

St. Louis City is an independent city (not part of any county) and the urban core of the St. Louis metro area. Cases are filed in the Eastern District of Missouri.

District: Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division)

Courthouse: 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

St. Louis City bankruptcy guide →

St. Louis County

St. Louis County surrounds St. Louis City and is the most populous county in the metro area, including suburbs like Clayton, Kirkwood, and University City. Cases are filed in the Eastern District of Missouri.

District: Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division)

Courthouse: 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

St. Louis County bankruptcy guide →

St. Charles County

St. Charles County is west of St. Louis across the Missouri River and is one of the fastest-growing counties in the metro area. Includes the cities of St. Charles, O'Fallon, and St. Peters.

District: Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division)

Courthouse: 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

St. Charles County bankruptcy guide →

Jefferson County

Jefferson County is south of St. Louis and includes the cities of Arnold, Festus, and Hillsboro. It is part of the Eastern District of Missouri.

District: Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division)

Courthouse: 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

Jefferson County bankruptcy guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which counties are in the St. Louis bankruptcy metro area?

The St. Louis metro area covers 4 counties: St. Louis City, St. Louis County, St. Charles County, Jefferson County. Each county falls under a specific federal bankruptcy court district.

Does it matter which county I live in for bankruptcy?

Yes. Your county determines which federal district court handles your case, and potentially which state exemptions apply. Different districts may have different local rules, trustees, and dismissal rates.

## Bankruptcy in St. Louis City -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/counties/st-louis-city.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » St. Louis City

Bankruptcy in St. Louis City

St. Louis City is an independent city (not part of any county) and the urban core of the St. Louis metro area. Cases are filed in the Eastern District of Missouri.

Key Information

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division)

All bankruptcy cases from St. Louis City are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Missouri Exemptions in St. Louis City

Missouri requires use of state exemptions. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. Wages: 75% of earned but unpaid wages exempt (90% if head of household). Retirement accounts: fully exempt. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in St. Louis City

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- The petition is filed with the Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles St. Louis City cases?

St. Louis City cases are filed in the Eastern District of Missouri, St. Louis Division. The courthouse is at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. Note that St. Louis City is an independent city, separate from St. Louis County.

What is Missouri's homestead exemption?

Missouri's homestead exemption protects only $15,000 of equity in your home -- one of the lower amounts in the nation. However, the wildcard exemption ($600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead) can provide additional protection for other assets.

Is St. Louis City different from St. Louis County for bankruptcy?

Both are in the Eastern District of Missouri, so the same court handles cases from both. The difference is political -- St. Louis City is an independent city, not part of St. Louis County. The same Missouri exemptions apply to both.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Other St. Louis Area Counties

St. Louis County
St. Charles County
Jefferson County
All Counties →

## Bankruptcy in St. Louis County -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/counties/st-louis-county.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » St. Louis County

Bankruptcy in St. Louis County

St. Louis County surrounds St. Louis City and is the most populous county in the metro area, including suburbs like Clayton, Kirkwood, and University City. Cases are filed in the Eastern District of Missouri.

Key Information

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division)

All bankruptcy cases from St. Louis County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Missouri Exemptions in St. Louis County

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus unused homestead up to $3,250. Wages: 75% exempt (90% for head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. Federal exemptions not available.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in St. Louis County

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- The petition is filed with the Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do St. Louis County residents file bankruptcy?

St. Louis County cases are filed in the Eastern District of Missouri, St. Louis Division. The courthouse is at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102 -- the same courthouse used for St. Louis City cases.

Can I keep my home in St. Louis County if I file bankruptcy?

Missouri's homestead exemption is $15,000. If your home equity is below $15,000, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home regardless of equity while catching up on missed payments. Given St. Louis County home values, many homeowners have equity exceeding $15,000, making Chapter 13 the safer option for homeowners.

What is the Chapter 13 success rate in the Eastern District?

The Eastern District of Missouri has a historically high Chapter 13 dismissal rate. Quality legal representation and a realistic budget are critical to completing a Chapter 13 plan in this district.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in St. Charles County -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/counties/st-charles-county.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » St. Charles County

Bankruptcy in St. Charles County

St. Charles County is west of St. Louis across the Missouri River and is one of the fastest-growing counties in the metro area. Includes the cities of St. Charles, O'Fallon, and St. Peters.

Key Information

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division)

All bankruptcy cases from St. Charles County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Missouri Exemptions in St. Charles County

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% head of household). Retirement accounts: fully exempt.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in St. Charles County

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- The petition is filed with the Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which court handles St. Charles County bankruptcies?

St. Charles County cases are filed in the Eastern District of Missouri, St. Louis Division, at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. Despite being a separate county west of the Missouri River, it falls in the same federal district as St. Louis City and County.

Is St. Charles County in the same bankruptcy district as St. Louis?

Yes. St. Charles County is part of the Eastern District of Missouri, the same district as St. Louis City and St. Louis County. Your case will be handled at the St. Louis courthouse and assigned to the same pool of bankruptcy judges.

What are typical attorney fees in St. Charles County?

Attorney fees in the St. Louis metro area typically range from $1,000-$1,800 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13. Fees are generally similar across St. Charles, St. Louis County, and the City. Chapter 13 attorney fees are subject to court approval.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Jefferson County -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/counties/jefferson-county.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Jefferson County

Bankruptcy in Jefferson County

Jefferson County is south of St. Louis and includes the cities of Arnold, Festus, and Hillsboro. It is part of the Eastern District of Missouri.

Key Information

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Jefferson County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Missouri Exemptions in Jefferson County

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% if head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. No federal exemptions in Missouri.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Jefferson County

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- The petition is filed with the Eastern District of Missouri (St. Louis Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Jefferson County residents file bankruptcy?

Jefferson County cases are filed in the Eastern District of Missouri, St. Louis Division. The courthouse is at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. Jefferson County residents typically drive 30-45 minutes to the St. Louis courthouse for hearings.

Does Jefferson County have different exemptions than St. Louis?

No. Missouri exemptions are the same statewide. The $15,000 homestead exemption, $3,000 vehicle exemption, and all other Missouri exemptions apply equally in Jefferson County, St. Louis County, and everywhere else in Missouri.

Can I file bankruptcy to stop a foreclosure in Jefferson County?

Yes. Filing any chapter of bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately stops foreclosure proceedings. Chapter 13 is typically the best option for saving a home because it allows you to catch up on missed mortgage payments over 3-5 years while keeping your property.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

St. Louis County

## Find Your Bankruptcy District -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/district-lookup.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Find Your Bankruptcy District

Enter your zip code to find which federal bankruptcy court handles cases in your area of the St. Louis metro. The metro spans two states and multiple districts.

Zip Code Lookup

Look Up

St. Louis Metro Bankruptcy Districts

Eastern District of Missouri (E.D. Mo.)

Covers St. Louis and eastern Missouri including St. Louis City, St. Louis County, St. Charles, Jefferson, Franklin, and surrounding counties. Zip codes typically in the 630xx-631xx and 636xx-639xx ranges.

Courthouse: 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

Western District of Missouri (W.D. Mo.)

Covers Kansas City and western Missouri. Zip codes typically starting with 640xx-648xx and other 64xxx codes. If you are in the western part of the state, you file here.

Courthouse: 400 E 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

Southern District of Illinois (S.D. Ill.)

Covers the Illinois side of the St. Louis metro (East St. Louis, Belleville, Granite City, Collinsville, Alton). Zip codes starting with 620xx-629xx.

Courthouse: 750 Missouri Ave, East St. Louis, IL 62201

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy district covers St. Louis?

St. Louis is in the Eastern District of Missouri. The courthouse is located at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. Most St. Louis metro zip codes on the Missouri side (630xx-631xx, 636xx-639xx) fall within this district.

What about the Illinois side of the metro?

If you live on the Illinois side of the St. Louis metro (East St. Louis, Belleville, Granite City, etc.), you file in the Southern District of Illinois. Zip codes starting with 620xx-629xx are typically in S.D. Ill. The courthouse is at 750 Missouri Ave, East St. Louis, IL 62201.

What is the difference between Eastern and Western District of Missouri?

Missouri has two federal bankruptcy districts. The Eastern District covers St. Louis and eastern Missouri (zip codes 630xx-631xx, 636xx-639xx). The Western District covers Kansas City and western Missouri (zip codes 640xx-648xx, 64xxx). The dividing line runs roughly through the center of the state.

Other City Bankruptcy Guides

Houston
Miami
Phoenix
Minneapolis
Kansas City
New York
Newark
San Antonio

## Free Legal Help for Bankruptcy in St. Louis | St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/legal-aid.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Free Legal Help for Bankruptcy in St. Louis

If you are struggling with debt but cannot afford a bankruptcy attorney, these St. Louis-area organizations may be able to help -- for free or at reduced cost.

St. Louis Legal Aid Organizations

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri

Phone: (314) 534-4200

Website: lsem.org

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri provides free civil legal assistance to low-income residents of the St. Louis metropolitan area and eastern Missouri. They handle a wide range of consumer and debt-related matters, including bankruptcy guidance and representation for qualifying individuals.

St. Louis Volunteer Lawyers

Phone: (314) 534-4200

St. Louis Volunteer Lawyers connects low-income residents with volunteer attorneys who provide free legal help. Their programs cover a variety of civil legal matters, and they may be able to match you with an attorney experienced in bankruptcy filings.

Land of Lincoln Legal Aid

Website: landoflegal.org

Land of Lincoln Legal Aid serves low-income residents on the Illinois side of the St. Louis metro area (Metro East). If you live in Madison, St. Clair, or other Illinois counties near St. Louis, Land of Lincoln may be able to help with bankruptcy and debt-related matters. They provide free legal assistance across central and southern Illinois.

Who Qualifies for Legal Aid?

Most legal aid organizations serve people whose household income is at or below 125-200% of the federal poverty level. For reference, the 2026 guidelines are approximately:

Single person: $15,060 (100% FPL) -- up to ~$30,120 (200% FPL)

Family of 2: $20,440 -- up to ~$40,880

Family of 4: $31,200 -- up to ~$62,400

Each organization sets its own income limits. Even if you are slightly above the threshold, it is worth calling -- some programs make exceptions based on circumstances such as medical emergencies, domestic violence, or sudden job loss.

Filing on Your Own (Pro Se)

If you do not qualify for legal aid but cannot afford an attorney, you have the right to file bankruptcy on your own. This is called filing "pro se." It is more common in Chapter 7 cases, which are generally simpler than Chapter 13.

The Pro Se Bankruptcy Guide provides step-by-step instructions for people filing without an attorney.

You can also reduce costs by:

Requesting a filing fee waiver (Chapter 7 only, if income is below 150% FPL)

Paying the filing fee in installments

Using free credit counseling agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee

Pro Se Filing Guide

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I get free bankruptcy help in St. Louis?

Yes. Several organizations in St. Louis provide free legal assistance for bankruptcy cases, including Legal Services of Eastern Missouri and St. Louis Volunteer Lawyers. Eligibility is generally based on income, typically at or below 125-200% of the federal poverty level.

Do I qualify for legal aid?

Most legal aid organizations in St. Louis serve people whose income is at or below 125-200% of the federal poverty level. For a single person in 2026, that is roughly $18,000-$30,000 per year. Family size affects the threshold. Contact the organization directly for specific eligibility requirements.

What if I make too much for legal aid?

If your income is too high for legal aid, consider filing pro se (on your own) using resources at prosebankruptcy.org. Some attorneys offer payment plans or flat-fee arrangements. If you live on the Illinois side of the metro area, also check Land of Lincoln Legal Aid.

What documents do I need to apply for free bankruptcy help?

Most legal aid organizations will ask for proof of income (pay stubs, tax returns, benefits letters), identification, and a summary of your debts and assets. Bring as much documentation as you can to your first appointment to speed up the process.

Browse Other Cities

Houston
Miami
Phoenix
Minneapolis
Kansas City
New York
Newark
San Antonio

Bankruptcy by City -- St. Louis Area

Find bankruptcy information specific to your city or suburb in the St. Louis metro area. This is a cross-state metro -- Missouri and Illinois suburbs file in different federal courts with different exemptions. Each page includes the correct courthouse, exemptions, and local resources.

Select Your City

Click on your city below for courthouse location, exemptions, zip codes, local resources, and frequently asked questions specific to your community.

## Bankruptcy in St. Charles, MO -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/cities/st-charles.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » St. Charles, MO

Bankruptcy in St. Charles, MO

St. Charles is the county seat of St. Charles County and one of the oldest cities on the Missouri River. Located about 30 miles west of downtown St. Louis, it is a rapidly growing suburban community.

Key Information

County

St. Charles County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri

All bankruptcy cases from St. Charles are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

63301
63302
63303
63304

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

Missouri Exemptions in St. Charles

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% for head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in St. Charles, MO

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- Filed with the Eastern District of Missouri. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in St. Charles

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri -- Free legal help for qualifying low-income residents

St. Charles County Community Resources -- Financial counseling and assistance programs

Catholic Charities of St. Louis -- Financial counseling and emergency assistance

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do St. Charles residents file bankruptcy?

St. Charles residents file in the Eastern District of Missouri at the Thomas F. Eagleton U.S. Courthouse, 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. The drive from St. Charles is approximately 35-40 minutes east on I-70.

What is the homestead exemption in St. Charles?

Missouri's homestead exemption is only $15,000 -- one of the lowest in the nation. Given higher home values in St. Charles, many homeowners have equity exceeding this amount. Chapter 13 may be the better option to protect your home.

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy in St. Charles?

Missouri's vehicle exemption is $3,000. If your car equity is under $3,000, you can keep it in Chapter 7. For vehicles worth more, you may use the wildcard exemption ($600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead) to protect additional equity.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Other St. Louis Area Cities

Webster Groves, MO
Belleville, IL

## Bankruptcy in O'Fallon, MO -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/cities/ofallon.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » O'Fallon, MO

Bankruptcy in O'Fallon, MO

O'Fallon is the largest city in St. Charles County and one of the fastest-growing cities in Missouri. Located about 35 miles west of downtown St. Louis along I-70, it has a strong suburban economy.

Key Information

County

St. Charles County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri

All bankruptcy cases from O'Fallon are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

63366
63368

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

Missouri Exemptions in O'Fallon

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% for head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in O'Fallon, MO

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- Filed with the Eastern District of Missouri. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in O'Fallon

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri -- Free legal help for qualifying low-income residents

St. Charles County Family Resource Center -- Financial counseling and community resources

Community Council of St. Charles County -- Emergency assistance and counseling

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do O'Fallon, MO residents file bankruptcy?

O'Fallon residents file in the Eastern District of Missouri at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. The drive is approximately 40-45 minutes east on I-70. Do not confuse with O'Fallon, IL, which files in a different federal district.

Is O'Fallon, MO different from O'Fallon, IL for bankruptcy?

Yes, completely. O'Fallon, MO is in St. Charles County and files in the Eastern District of Missouri under Missouri law. O'Fallon, IL is in St. Clair County and files in the Southern District of Illinois under Illinois law. Different exemptions, different courts, different trustees.

How do I qualify for Chapter 7 in O'Fallon?

You must pass the means test, which compares your income to the Missouri median. If your household income is below the median, you automatically qualify. If above, additional calculations for allowed expenses determine eligibility. Credit counseling is also required before filing.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Florissant, MO -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/cities/florissant.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Florissant, MO

Bankruptcy in Florissant, MO

Florissant is the largest city in St. Louis County by population, located in the northern part of the county. It is one of the oldest communities in the St. Louis metro area, founded in 1786.

Key Information

County

St. Louis County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri

All bankruptcy cases from Florissant are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

63031
63032
63033
63034

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

Missouri Exemptions in Florissant

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% for head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Florissant, MO

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- Filed with the Eastern District of Missouri. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Florissant

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri -- Free legal help for qualifying residents

North County Community Resources -- Financial counseling and emergency assistance

St. Louis County Library Legal Resources -- Free legal information and forms

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Florissant residents file bankruptcy?

Florissant residents file in the Eastern District of Missouri at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. The courthouse is approximately 20-25 minutes south in downtown St. Louis.

What is the cost of filing bankruptcy in Florissant?

Court filing fees are $338 for Chapter 7 and $313 for Chapter 13. Attorney fees in the St. Louis area typically range from $800-$2,000 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13. Fee waivers are available for those below 150% of the poverty line.

Can I stop wage garnishment by filing bankruptcy in Florissant?

Yes. The automatic stay immediately stops all wage garnishments when your bankruptcy petition is filed. In Missouri, creditors can garnish up to 25% of your disposable wages before bankruptcy. Filing stops this the same day.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Chesterfield, MO -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/cities/chesterfield.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Chesterfield, MO

Bankruptcy in Chesterfield, MO

Chesterfield is an affluent western suburb of St. Louis in St. Louis County. It is home to significant commercial development along the I-64/US-40 corridor and known for its high quality of life.

Key Information

County

St. Louis County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri

All bankruptcy cases from Chesterfield are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

63005
63017

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

Missouri Exemptions in Chesterfield

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% for head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Chesterfield, MO

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- Filed with the Eastern District of Missouri. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Chesterfield

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri -- Free legal help for qualifying residents

St. Louis County Department of Human Services -- Financial assistance programs

West County Community Resources -- Local assistance and referral services

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can high-income Chesterfield residents file bankruptcy?

Yes. High income does not disqualify you from bankruptcy. If you earn above the Missouri median, Chapter 13 is available regardless of income level. Some high-income filers may also qualify for Chapter 7 after means test expense deductions.

Will I lose my Chesterfield home in bankruptcy?

Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption is very low relative to Chesterfield home values. If your equity exceeds $15,000, Chapter 13 is likely the better option -- it allows you to keep your home while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Consult with an attorney about your specific equity situation.

How does bankruptcy affect my Chesterfield business?

If you own a business, bankruptcy options depend on the business structure. Sole proprietors can file personal Chapter 7 or 13. LLCs and corporations may file Chapter 11 (reorganization) or Chapter 7 (liquidation) separately. Business assets may or may not be protected depending on your structure.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Wildwood, MO -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/cities/wildwood.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Wildwood, MO

Bankruptcy in Wildwood, MO

Wildwood is the largest city by area in St. Louis County, located in the far western part of the county. It is known for its rural character, large lots, and outdoor recreation areas.

Key Information

County

St. Louis County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri

All bankruptcy cases from Wildwood are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

63005
63011
63021
63025
63038
63040
63069

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

Missouri Exemptions in Wildwood

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% for head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Wildwood, MO

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- Filed with the Eastern District of Missouri. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Wildwood

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri -- Free legal help for qualifying residents

St. Louis County Department of Human Services -- Financial counseling and assistance

Wildwood City Resources -- Local government referral services

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Wildwood residents file bankruptcy?

Wildwood residents file in the Eastern District of Missouri at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. The drive from Wildwood is approximately 35-40 minutes east on I-64/US-40.

Can I protect my acreage in Wildwood through bankruptcy?

Missouri's homestead exemption is $15,000 regardless of property size. This applies to the value of your interest, not the acreage. For properties with significant equity (common in Wildwood), Chapter 13 is typically the better option to protect your home.

What happens to my retirement accounts in bankruptcy?

Retirement accounts including 401(k)s, IRAs, pensions, and 403(b)s are fully exempt in Missouri bankruptcy. These accounts are protected regardless of their balance. This is one of the strongest protections available to Wildwood residents.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Ballwin, MO -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/cities/ballwin.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Ballwin, MO

Bankruptcy in Ballwin, MO

Ballwin is a western suburb of St. Louis in St. Louis County. It has been ranked as one of the best places to live in Missouri and offers a mix of suburban amenities and community character.

Key Information

County

St. Louis County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri

All bankruptcy cases from Ballwin are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

63011
63021

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

Missouri Exemptions in Ballwin

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% for head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Ballwin, MO

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- Filed with the Eastern District of Missouri. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Ballwin

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri -- Free legal help for qualifying residents

West County Community Resources -- Financial counseling and assistance programs

Catholic Charities of St. Louis -- Emergency financial assistance

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Ballwin residents file bankruptcy?

Ballwin residents file in the Eastern District of Missouri at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. The drive from Ballwin is approximately 25-30 minutes east on I-64/US-40.

What debts can be eliminated in bankruptcy?

Most unsecured debts are dischargeable: credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, utility bills, and some older tax debts. Non-dischargeable debts include recent taxes, student loans (unless undue hardship is proven), child support, and alimony. The same rules apply throughout Missouri.

Can I file bankruptcy without my spouse in Ballwin?

Yes. In Missouri, either spouse can file individually. However, if you have joint debts, the non-filing spouse remains liable. Filing jointly may provide better exemption protection -- married couples filing jointly can double most Missouri exemptions.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Maryland Heights, MO -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/cities/maryland-heights.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Maryland Heights, MO

Bankruptcy in Maryland Heights, MO

Maryland Heights is a suburban city in western St. Louis County, located along the I-270 corridor. It is home to the Hollywood Casino Amphitheatre and significant commercial development.

Key Information

County

St. Louis County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri

All bankruptcy cases from Maryland Heights are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

63043
63146

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

Missouri Exemptions in Maryland Heights

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% for head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Maryland Heights, MO

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- Filed with the Eastern District of Missouri. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Maryland Heights

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri -- Free legal help for qualifying residents

St. Louis County Family Resource Centers -- Financial counseling and referrals

United Way 211 St. Louis -- Dial 211 for local assistance referrals

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Maryland Heights residents file bankruptcy?

Maryland Heights residents file in the Eastern District of Missouri at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. The drive is approximately 20-25 minutes east on I-270 and I-64.

How does bankruptcy affect renting in Maryland Heights?

Landlords can see bankruptcy on credit reports. However, many landlords are willing to work with people who have filed bankruptcy, especially if you can demonstrate current income and stability. A bankruptcy discharge actually makes you a lower risk because you cannot file again for several years.

Can I keep my bank account if I file bankruptcy?

Yes. You can keep your bank account, but the trustee may look at the balance on the day you file. Keep balances reasonable -- do not withdraw large amounts before filing. Missouri's wildcard exemption ($600 plus up to $3,250 unused homestead) can protect bank account funds.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Kirkwood, MO -- St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://stlouisbankruptcy.org/cities/kirkwood.html

St. Louis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Kirkwood, MO

Bankruptcy in Kirkwood, MO

Kirkwood is an established inner-ring suburb of St. Louis in St. Louis County. Known for its walkable downtown, historic train station, and strong community identity, it is one of the oldest suburbs in the St. Louis area.

Key Information

County

St. Louis County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of Missouri

All bankruptcy cases from Kirkwood are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

63122

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

Missouri Exemptions in Kirkwood

Missouri state exemptions apply. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Household goods: $3,000. Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,250 of unused homestead. Wages: 75% exempt (90% for head of household). Retirement: fully exempt. Missouri does not allow federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Kirkwood, MO

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- Filed with the Eastern District of Missouri. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Kirkwood

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri -- Free legal help for qualifying residents

Kirkwood Community Resources -- Local government assistance and referrals

St. Louis Area Food Bank -- Emergency food assistance

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Kirkwood residents file bankruptcy?

Kirkwood residents file in the Eastern District of Missouri at 111 S 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102. The courthouse is approximately 15-20 minutes east in downtown St. Louis.

What happens to my Kirkwood home equity in bankruptcy?

Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption is low relative to Kirkwood home values. If you have more than $15,000 in equity, the Chapter 7 trustee could sell your home to pay creditors. Chapter 13 is usually the better option for Kirkwood homeowners with equity, allowing you to keep your home.

How soon can I rebuild credit after bankruptcy?

Many filers begin rebuilding credit immediately after discharge using secured credit cards. Most people see credit score improvements within 12-18 months. You can typically qualify for a car loan within 1-2 years and a mortgage within 2-4 years after discharge.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
